note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries into caption-sized pieces (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered). Block windows are MEASURED onsets (FFT cross-correlation of each WC-04-01-sNN.mp3 against the joined master): the nominal Sigma(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula sums to 218.6465s, overshooting the ffprobe-probed master (218.2008s) by 0.446s (> 0.3 tolerance) because ffprobe's per-file mp3 durations overstate decoded sample counts. Measured onsets land exactly at Sigma(decoded_audio+0.4) (delta +0.000 for every block; decoded master 218.16s). audioDurationS in the lesson JSON is the probed master 218.201s.

phrases.text: Open Inventory, then Inventory Items.
This is the master catalog of everything a site stocks and sells off the shelf.
Tunnel chemicals, counter supplies, the equipment behind them.
The rest of the inventory pages wait in the sidebar for the next lesson.
Start with the four tiles up top.
Total items, low stock, categories, vendors.
The one that earns a daily glance is low stock.
It counts what's running thin before a cashier tells you the shelf is empty.
Below them, three quick reads.
A twelve-month count trend, the mix by category,
and the split between active, low, and discontinued stock.
You size up the whole catalog here before scrolling a single row,
which is often all a quick stock question needs.
Now the list itself.
New Record builds an item from scratch; that's a training exercise, not for today.
Export ships the data out, so leave it alone.
Filters and search are how you find one item in a long catalog.
Read across the columns.
Two IDs, two prices, a count, a type, a vendor.
The item ID is WashCentral's own number;
the S-K-U is the stock code you match against a shipment.
Cost is what you pay, price is what the customer pays.
Click the first row to open its record.
The header names the item, badges it as an inventory item,
and tags it with type and stock chips,
so you know what you're holding before you read a field.
One row, one full record behind it.
Look top right: three buttons.
Back, Delete, and Edit Record.
Back is the only one we use.
Delete removes a record from a live shared tenant, so it never gets clicked in training.
Edit Record opens the editing flow, and editing is its own workflow with its own rules.
Find all three, then leave two of them alone.
The record splits its fields across three tabs.
Item details, pricing and stock, storage and location.
The legacy Pricing and Tax and Commission and Service names are gone;
this is the layout you'll actually see on the floor.
Start on Item Details.
Eight read-only fields, mirroring the eight columns you just left.
The item's two IDs, its description, what it costs, what it sells for,
forty two units on hand, its type, and its vendor.
That is the ticket answer in one card.
What do we charge, and how many are left.
Open the Pricing and Stock tab.
Watch what changes.
The heading does.
The fields underneath do not.
The same eight facts render again, top to bottom.
That repetition is what this tenant actually shows today, and it is logged,
so do not hunt for pricing-only fields that are not there.
Then Storage and Location.
Same pattern.
A new heading, the identical eight fields, and no bin, aisle, or site anywhere on the page.
So treat the tabs as navigation and learn the eight fields once.
If storage detail lands here on a later build, a future pass will pick it up.
Change nothing, then click Back.
The detail page closes and drops you back on the list, the record exactly as you found it.
On a shared live tenant that discipline matters.
Reading answers the ticket, and Back guarantees you left no mark.
That's Inventory Items end to end.
Counts and trends up top, eight columns across the list,
and a three-tab record behind every row.
You read all of it and change none of it,
which is exactly how a stock question should be answered.
